retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Contraindicated Chemotherapeutic Agents -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Doxorubicin is a cytotoxic anthracycline antibiotic that intercalates between base pairs of the DNA/RNA strand, inhibiting DNA and RNA synthesis, thereby preventing replication of rapidly growing cells. Doxorubicin is used in the treatment regimens for various cancers, some of which include: lymphomas, breast cancer, and sarcomas. Cardiomyopathy is the most crucial long-term toxicity, and it may manifest with acute or chronic effects. Acutely it may cause EKG changes with ST and T wave abnormalities and arrhythmias. Chronically it may progress to congestive heart failure, with this being cumulative dose-related toxicity. Doxorubicin is a contraindication with hyperbaric oxygen due to enhanced drug toxicity. An experimental study with rats produced data showing there was an 87% mortality rate in rats that were exposed to hyperbaric oxygen while concomitantly receiving doxorubicin, and it was postulated to be due to cardiac toxicity. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Sarcoma -- Complications -- Chemotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Dexrazoxane is the only FDA-approved drug for cardioprotection against anthracycline cardiotoxicity, but its use is restricted to patients with metastatic breast cancer who have received at least 300mg/m of doxorubicin and planned ongoing therapy. It was the only drug in a large meta-analysis to reduce the risk of heart failure in this patient population. [59] If anthracyclines are given after radiotherapy, then patients may experience radiation recall, resulting in blistering and peeling of the skin.

retrieved_docs.title: Anthracycline Medications (Doxorubicin) -- Toxicity -- Toxicity Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: A retrospective study found that dexrazoxane did not significantly affect the mean LVEF after anthracycline administration (56% with dexrazoxane vs. 53% without), but while the authors concluded that it decreased the rate of cardiac events, defined as a reduction of LVEF more than 10%, it is difficult to reconcile that conclusion of this retrospective analysis with the fact that the mean LVEF between the two groups was not different. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Anthracycline Medications (Doxorubicin) -- Toxicity -- Toxicity Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: In a meta-analysis reviewing 16 trials, including 1918 patients: dexrazoxane reduced cardiotoxicity after anthracycline treatment by 33%. It specifically decreased heart failure by 58% compared to other agents, including beta-blockers, statins, coenzyme Q-10, and N-acetylcysteine. However, the study determined dexrazoxane did not affect the response rate of malignancies or the risk of death. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Electron Transport Chain -- Clinical Significance -- Oxidative Phosphorylation Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Doxorubicin – coenzyme Q (theoretical) Doxorubicin is used in cancer chemotherapy, typically breast and bladder carcinomas, and lymphoma. A well-known side effect of doxorubicin is dilated cardiomyopathy. One proposed mechanism of causation is the generation of reactive oxygen species within myocardial tissue as the drug interferes with electron transfer by coenzyme Q. [27] Antimycin A – inhibits complex III (cytochrome c reductase) Antimycin A is a piscicide that binds to cytochrome c reductase at the Qi binding site. This activity prevents ubiquinone from binding and accepting an electron, thereby blocking the recycling of ubiquinol (CoQH2) by the Q cycle. Carbon Monoxide (CO) – inhibits complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase)

retrieved_docs.title: Anthracyclines -- Contraindications -- Relative Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracycline hypersensitivity Patients with previous anaphylactic reactions following administration of anthracyclines Hepatic disease Patients with severe hepatic disease, defined as Child-Pugh class C or serum bilirubin greater than 5 mg/dL. Dosage adjustments are necessary for patients with mild to moderate hepatic disease. Pre-existing cardiac conditions Heart failure, cardiomyopathy, myocardial infarction, myocarditis, pericarditis Assessment of cardiac function using a MUGA or echocardiogram is necessary before starting treatment in these patients. Gender Females have a higher risk of anthracycline-induced cardiotoxicity Age Children and adolescents have a higher risk of developing late cardiovascular dysfunction. They should receive periodic cardiac evaluations. Reports of prepubertal growth inhibition/failure and gonadal impairment in children and adolescents Pregnancy (category D) In animal studies Breastfeeding Live vaccines

retrieved_docs.title: Anthracycline Toxicity -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: There are detailed guidelines to improve outcomes for patients undergoing anthracycline chemotherapy, particularly in those with risk factors for poorer outcomes. [30] The highest quality evidence primarily focuses on patients with high or very high risk of developing anthracycline toxicity; the cardiac physiologist is critical in deriving cardiac imaging for further decision-making, with regular cardiac imaging recommended for those at the highest risk. The formulation of additional evidence is challenged by the high mortality rate associated with the primary malignancy. In all patients with significant anthracycline toxicity, the early commencement of heart failure therapy aids significantly in reducing morbidity and mortality. Newer anticancer derivatives of anthracycline-class medications and liposomal formulations can reduce the incidence of adverse outcomes in anthracycline chemotherapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Anthracycline Medications (Doxorubicin) -- Toxicity -- Toxicity Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: Several pharmacological agents have been used and studied to prevent and/or decrease cardiotoxicity in patients receiving anthracyclines, including dexrazoxane, beta-blockers, angiotensin antagonists, statins coenzyme Q-10, and N-acetylcysteine. Most notable studies have taken place testing beta-blockers and dexrazoxane.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2912
retrieved_docs.content: The GDC Value Terminology is a structured terminology that incorporates several pharmacologic concepts, including the chemotherapeutic agent paclitaxel, the class of inhibitory protonové pumpy (proton pump inhibitors), the cardiac glycoside digoxin, and the phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor roflumilast. By grouping these diverse drugs—paclitaxel for cancer treatment, digoxin for heart failure and arrhythmias, roflumilast for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and inhibitory protonové pumpy for gastric acid suppression—GDC Value Terminology provides a unified framework for representing medication data across oncology, cardiology, pulmonology, and gastroenterology, facilitating consistent documentation, data exchange, and clinical decision support.

retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac glycosides constitute a pharmacologic class of compounds that act on the heart to increase contractility and are commonly employed in the treatment of heart failure and certain arrhythmias; within this class, strofantiny is identified as a specific cardiac glycoside, representing a distinct member of the broader group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 717
retrieved_docs.content: The community recognizes that immune function, metabolic pathways, and urinary excretion are tightly linked and are influenced by a broad spectrum of pharmacologic agents and disease states. PARP‑inhibitory activity, antacids, tuberculostatic antibiotics, antineoplastic (anti‑cancer) agents, antinematodal agents, antipsychotics such as acepromazine, bismuth compounds, chymotrypsin, ethanolamine, hypothalamic hormones, hypoxanthine, insulin lispro, potassium citrate (Kaliumcitrát), colesevelam, linkosamides, various peptides and spiro‑containing compounds are identified as modifiers that may require immune monitoring or metabolic assessment. Clinically, conditions including urinary‑tract calculus, cardiac arrest with ventricular fibrillation, esophageal disorders (with the esophagus serving as the direct target for upper gastrointestinal x‑ray examinations), and non‑Hodgkin lymphoma (ورم الغدد اللمفاوية (غير هودجكين)) are highlighted as contexts in which immune status, metabolism, and urinary biomarkers are particularly relevant. Urine is described as a source of diagnostic peptides, spiro‑type metabolites, the herbal substance “سعفة الرأس,” and tissue fragments from non‑Hodgkin lymphoma, reflecting its utility in monitoring the effects of the aforementioned agents and diseases. Potassium citrate’s ability to inhibit urinary stone formation underscores its role in prophylaxis against urinary calculi. Overall, the integration of immunologic assessment, metabolic regulation, and urinary analysis across these agents, anatomical sites, acute events, and benign or malignant diseases is emphasized as essential for accurate diagnosis, therapeutic planning, and patient monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1426
retrieved_docs.content: Aspirin and digoxin are frequently noted to co‑occur in clinical practice, indicating that these two medications are often prescribed together or observed concurrently in patients, particularly in the management of cardiovascular conditions where antiplatelet therapy with aspirin may be combined with the cardiac glycoside effects of digoxin. This co‑occurrence highlights the importance of monitoring for potential drug interactions and ensuring coordinated care when both agents are part of a patient’s treatment regimen.

retrieved_docs.content: and cyclopentolate) interact with both immune and metabolic processes. Herbal preparations such as silymarin, head fern (سعفة الرأس) and lemongrass are detectable in urine and may affect immune signaling. Urine is an important biological matrix for monitoring hormonal levels, enzymatic activity, drug exposure and disease markers, enabling detection and delivery of diagnostic and therapeutic agents including chenodeoxycholic acid, omalizumab (a monoclonal anti‑IgE antibody), thyroglobulin (a thyroid antigen and autoimmune target), puberty markers, various peptides and ethanolamine. Clinical entities—diseases of white blood cells, urinary‑tract calculi, cardiac arrest, ventricular fibrillation and non‑Hodgkin lymphoma (ورم الغدد اللمفاوية غير هودجكين)—trigger inflammatory responses or require immune‑directed therapies.
